nodes.name: Word Origins
nodes.description: English words borrowed from other languages

items.prompt: What language gives us the word 'algorithm,' named after the 9th-century mathematician al-Khwarizmi?
items.explanation:
From the Latinized name 'Algoritmi,' derived from Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi
Al-Khwarizmi was a Persian scholar working in Baghdad's House of Wisdom (~820 AD)
His book on Hindu-Arabic numerals was translated into Latin as Algoritmi de numero Indorum
The Latin text begins 'Dixit Algorismi' ('Thus spoke al-Khwarizmi'), giving us the English word
Al-Khwarizmi also wrote the foundational algebra text Kitab al-Jabr, giving us the word 'algebra'
His name means 'the native of Khwarezm' (modern Khiva, Uzbekistan)
The word now describes any step-by-step computational procedure

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'tsunami' come, meaning 'harbor wave'?
items.explanation:
From Japanese tsu (津, 'harbor') + nami (波, 'wave')
Fishermen returning to port would find the harbor devastated despite calm seas offshore
Entered English usage after the 1896 Meiji Sanriku earthquake and tsunami
The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami killed ~230,000 people across 14 countries and made the word universally known
Before 'tsunami,' English used 'tidal wave,' though tsunamis are unrelated to tides
Caused by undersea earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides displacing water
Japan's Pacific coastline makes it especially vulnerable; the country has extensive early-warning systems

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'kindergarten' come, literally meaning 'children's garden'?
items.explanation:
From German Kinder ('children') + Garten ('garden')
Coined in 1840 by Friedrich Fröbel, a German education reformer
Fröbel opened his first school in 1837 in Blankenburg, Germany, initially called 'The Institution for Play and Occupation'
He likened children to plants that need nurturing in a garden-like environment
Fröbel's educational philosophy emphasized guided play and structured activities
The first U.S. kindergarten was opened in Watertown, Wisconsin, in 1856 by Margarethe Schurz
The word entered English unchanged, preserving the German compound noun structure

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'safari' come, originally meaning 'journey'?
items.explanation:
From Swahili safari meaning 'journey,' itself from Arabic safar (سفر) meaning 'travel' or 'trip'
Arab traders brought the word to the East African coast through centuries of trade
Entered English in the late 1850s, popularized by explorer Richard Francis Burton
Originally referred to any long overland journey in East Africa, not just wildlife viewing
The verb 'to travel' in Swahili is kusafiri, from the same Arabic root
Modern usage narrowed to mean a wildlife-viewing expedition, especially in Kenya and Tanzania
Apple's web browser Safari (2003) borrowed the word for its connotation of exploration

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'robot' come, introduced in a 1920 science-fiction play?
items.explanation:
Introduced in Karel Čapek's 1920 play R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)
From Czech robota meaning 'forced labor' or 'drudgery,' from rab ('slave')
Karel Čapek credited his brother Josef with actually coining the word
Čapek originally wanted to call them 'laboři' (from Latin labor) but found it too artificial
The play premiered January 2, 1921, in Hradec Králové; translated into 30 languages by 1923
In the play, the robots are biological, not mechanical — closer to modern clones or androids
The word displaced older terms like 'automaton' and 'android' in languages worldwide

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'shampoo' come, originally meaning 'to press or knead'?
items.explanation:
From Hindi chāmpo (चाँपो), imperative of chāmpnā ('to press, knead the muscles')
Ultimately from Sanskrit chapati (चपति) meaning 'to press or knead'
Entered English around 1762 during the British colonial era in India
Originally referred to a therapeutic head-and-body massage, not hair washing
The meaning shifted to 'lather and wash the hair' by the 1860s
British traders encountered the champi massage in Indian bathhouses and adopted the word
Sake Dean Mahomed, an Indian traveler, opened shampooing baths in Brighton, England, in 1814

items.prompt: From which language family does the word 'ketchup' ultimately derive, referring to a fermented fish sauce?
items.explanation:
From Hokkien Chinese kê-tsiáp or kôe-chiap meaning 'preserved fish sauce'
Passed through Malay as kicap/kecap before reaching English in the early 18th century
Original ketchup was a thin, dark fish sauce — nothing like modern tomato ketchup
British merchant Charles Lockyer described acquiring ketchup in Asia in 1711
Tomatoes were added to the recipe in the U.S. in the early 19th century
Heinz began selling tomato ketchup in 1876, eventually dominating the market
In Indonesia, kecap manis (sweet soy sauce) preserves the original Malay spelling

items.prompt: The word 'typhoon' has a complex origin, blending Greek, Arabic, and which Asian language?
items.explanation:
A convergence of three etymological streams: Greek, Arabic, and Chinese
Cantonese taaifung (大風, 'big wind') was heard by European sailors in the 16th–18th centuries
Arabic tūfān (طوفان, 'flood/deluge') appears in two passages of the Quran
Greek Typhon was a mythological storm giant, father of the winds
Portuguese tufão (attested by 1560) was the first European form of the word
The modern English spelling 'typhoon' first appeared in Shelley's Prometheus Unbound (1819)
The three unrelated words converged by coincidence into a single English term

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'yogurt' come?
items.explanation:
From Turkish yoğurt, related to the verb yoğurmak ('to knead' or 'to be curdled')
Fermented milk products have been made in Central Asia and the Middle East for thousands of years
Genghis Khan's armies are said to have subsisted partly on fermented mare's milk
The first industrialized production of yogurt began in Barcelona in 1919 by Isaac Carasso (Danone)
Stamen Grigorov, a Bulgarian physician, identified Lactobacillus bulgaricus in yogurt in 1905
The spelling varies widely: yogurt (U.S.), yoghurt (UK), yaourt (French), jogurt (German)
Turkish cuisine uses yogurt in both savory dishes (cacik, iskender) and drinks (ayran)

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'algebra' come, meaning 'the reunion of broken parts'?
items.explanation:
From Arabic al-jabr (الجبر), meaning 'the restoration' or 'reunion of broken parts'
Title of al-Khwarizmi's ~820 AD book Kitab al-Jabr wa-l-Muqabala
The same mathematician whose name gave us 'algorithm'
Al-jabr originally referred to a specific operation: moving a subtracted term to the other side of an equation
The book was translated into Latin by Robert of Chester in 1145, spreading algebra to Europe
Al-Khwarizmi's work synthesized Greek, Indian, and Babylonian mathematical traditions
The word entered English via Medieval Latin and Old Spanish algebrista (also a bone-setter)

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'sofa' come, originally meaning a raised platform with cushions?
items.explanation:
From Arabic suffa (صُفّة), meaning a raised platform or bench covered with cushions and rugs
The Ahl al-Suffa ('People of the Bench') were companions of the Prophet Muhammad who lived on a raised platform in his mosque
Entered European languages in the 17th century via Turkish, as the Ottoman Empire influenced European furnishings
Originally referred to a part of the floor raised a foot or two, covered with rich carpets and cushions
The modern meaning of an upholstered seat with a back and arms developed in the 18th century
The related word 'divan' also came from Arabic/Persian/Turkish into European languages
French adopted 'sofa' by 1657; English followed by 1625

items.prompt: From which indigenous American language does the word 'chocolate' derive?
items.explanation:
Likely from Nahuatl chocolatl or chicolati, meaning 'chocolate beverage'
The exact etymology is debated; possibly from 'bitter water' (xococ + atl) or 'cacao-beater' (chicol + atl)
The Aztecs drank chocolate as a cold, bitter, frothy beverage spiced with chili and vanilla
Cacao beans were so valuable the Aztecs used them as currency
Spanish conquistadors brought chocolate to Europe after encountering it in Mexico around 1519
The addition of sugar by the Spanish transformed it into a sweet drink, then a solid confection
Theobroma cacao, the scientific name for the cacao tree, means 'food of the gods' in Greek

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'piano' come, short for 'pianoforte' meaning 'soft-loud'?
items.explanation:
Short for pianoforte, from Italian piano ('soft') + forte ('loud')
Named for its ability to vary volume based on how hard the keys are struck
The first piano was built around 1700 by Bartolomeo Cristofori in Florence, Italy
Cristofori called it gravicembalo col piano e forte ('harpsichord with soft and loud')
Replaced the harpsichord, which could not vary dynamics
Piano and forte are also standard musical dynamics terms (p = soft, f = loud)
The musical meaning of piano derives from Latin planus ('flat, even, smooth')

items.prompt: From which language does the word 'karate' come, meaning 'empty hand'?
items.explanation:
From Japanese kara (空, 'empty') + te (手, 'hand')
Originally written with characters meaning 'Chinese hand' (唐手), reflecting its Okinawan and Chinese roots
Gichin Funakoshi changed the characters to 'empty hand' in the 1930s to make it more acceptable in Japan
Developed in the Ryukyu Kingdom (Okinawa) from indigenous fighting and Chinese kung fu influences
Became an Olympic sport at the 2020 Tokyo Games, though it was dropped for the 2024 Paris Games
Major styles include Shotokan, Goju-ryu, Shito-ryu, and Wado-ryu
The Karate Kid (1984) popularized the martial art in American culture; 'wax on, wax off' is iconic

items.prompt: From which ancient language does the word 'guru' come, meaning 'heavy' or 'weighty'?
items.explanation:
From Sanskrit guru (गुरु) meaning 'heavy' or 'weighty,' implying gravitas and authority
Cognate with Latin gravis ('heavy, serious') and Greek barus ('heavy')
A popular folk etymology splits it into gu ('darkness') + ru ('dispeller'), meaning 'dispeller of ignorance'
In Hinduism, Sikhism, and Buddhism, a guru is a spiritual teacher of highest importance
The concept of guru-shishya (teacher-disciple) tradition is central to Indian knowledge transmission
Entered English by 1613; the modern informal sense of 'expert' developed in the 20th century
Sikh tradition reveres ten human gurus, from Guru Nanak (1469–1539) to Guru Gobind Singh (1666–1708)
